$description: 10 sample slide scenarios for internal benchmarking of planning quality. Each scenario defines the input conditions and expected output characteristics. Use these as regression targets when changing the planning chain.

scenarios.name: Q1 Board Review — Revenue KPI Slide
scenarios.source_input: Q1 financial PDF with revenue, margin, and growth figures
scenarios.goal: Communicate Q1 revenue performance in a single KPI slide
success_notes: Headline states the revenue figure AND the growth rate, not just 'Revenue Performance'
Three KPI blocks: Revenue ($29.3M), vs Plan (+4%), QoQ Growth (+6%)
Each KPI has a delta with comparison basis (YoY, vs Plan, vs Q4)
Speaker notes explain what drove the results, not just repeat the numbers
failure_notes: Headline is a topic label like 'Financial Highlights' instead of a takeaway
KPI blocks contain sentences instead of metric + value + delta triples
Missing citations on any of the three callout blocks
Speaker notes are empty or just restate the on-slide content

scenarios.name: Oracle HCM — Options Comparison Table
scenarios.source_input: Integration options analysis document with three evaluated approaches
scenarios.goal: Present a side-by-side comparison of three integration options
success_notes: Headline states which option wins: 'Hybrid Wins on 4 of 5 Criteria'
Table has 5 rows (criteria) and 4 columns (criterion + 3 options)
Cell content uses short labels (High/Medium/Low, dollar amounts, week counts)
No sentences in table cells — just data points
Speaker notes explain why hybrid loses on timeline but wins overall
failure_notes: Headline is neutral ('Comparison Matrix') instead of stating the conclusion
Table cells contain full sentences instead of short data labels
More than 8 rows — table is too dense for a single slide
Uses content.1col with bullets instead of table.full

scenarios.name: Product Launch — Objection Handling Cards
scenarios.goal: Equip sales reps to handle the top 3 customer objections
success_notes: Exactly 3 cards, one per objection
Each card has a 2-3 word title (the objection) and a 1-2 sentence response
Responses include specific proof points: dates, certifications, metrics
Speaker notes give the fuller talk track for each objection
failure_notes: Uses content.1col with a bullet list instead of cards
More than 3 cards — forces a template that can't fit them
Card responses are generic ('We handle this well') without proof points
Objections are framed from the seller's perspective instead of the buyer's

scenarios.name: Incident Retro — Timeline Slide
scenarios.source_input: Incident timeline document with minute-by-minute log
scenarios.goal: Show the incident timeline with detection, response, and resolution phases
success_notes: Table columns: Time, Event, Phase (Detection/Response/Resolution)
5-8 key events, not every log entry — this is a summary, not a dump
Headline states the total duration: '47-Minute Outage: Detection to Resolution'
Speaker notes call out the 14-minute detection gap as the key learning
failure_notes: Every log entry included — 30+ rows in the table
Uses bullets instead of a table for timestamped events
Headline is 'Incident Timeline' without stating the duration or key metric
No mention of detection time in speaker notes

scenarios.name: Q1 Board Review — Risk Escalation Slide
scenarios.source_input: Q1 PDF with risk register section
scenarios.goal: Escalate two operational risks with proposed mitigations and deadlines
success_notes: Exactly 3 bullets: Risk 1 + data, Risk 2 + data, Actions + deadlines
Each risk bullet includes the specific number (71% concentration, 23% CAC increase)
Action bullet includes owner and deadline (May 1, April 30)
Headline frames urgency: 'Two Risks Require Board Attention' not 'Risk Overview'
failure_notes: Risks described without quantification ('There is a supplier risk')
Actions listed without deadlines or owners
More than 3 bullets — splits focus
Uses kpi.3up for risks — KPIs are for metrics, not risk narratives

scenarios.name: Product Launch — Performance Benchmark Chart
scenarios.source_input: Product spec with benchmark data across 5 dataset sizes
scenarios.goal: Show that our product is faster than the competitor at every data scale
success_notes: Chart type is bar with 5 data points (1TB through 50TB)
Headline states the conclusion: 'We Win on Speed at Every Data Scale'
Citation footer includes the benchmark source (DataBench Labs)
Speaker notes explain that the gap widens at larger scales
failure_notes: Chart data presented as a bullet list instead of a visual chart
Headline is descriptive ('Performance Benchmark') instead of conclusive
Missing citation for the benchmark source
Two charts on one slide (e.g., speed AND cost)

scenarios.name: Oracle HCM — Section Divider
scenarios.source_input: Integration options analysis (context only — no data needed for divider)
scenarios.goal: Signal the transition from context slides to the three options deep-dive
success_notes: Headline is 2-4 words: 'Three Integration Options'
Tagline is one sentence framing the comparison criteria
No bullets, no data, no citations — this is a structural slide
Speaker notes provide the transition: 'Moving into the core analysis...'
failure_notes: Section divider contains content (bullet lists, data)
Uses content.1col instead of section.header
Headline is a full sentence instead of a section title
Missing from the deck entirely — deck jumps from context to options without a break

scenarios.name: Q1 Board Review — Hiring Plan Table
scenarios.source_input: Financial model with headcount plan by department
scenarios.goal: Present the H1 hiring plan with roles, timing, and cost for board approval
success_notes: Table columns: Team, Roles, Quarter, Cost
3 department rows plus a Total row
Headline includes both the total count and total cost: '18 Roles, $2.4M'
Speaker notes mention that the cost is within approved opex and approval enables May 1 recruiting
failure_notes: Hiring plan presented as bullets instead of a table
Table missing the Total row — board can't see the aggregate
Headline omits the cost ('H1 Hiring Plan' instead of '18 Roles, $2.4M')
More than 6 rows — too granular for a board slide (use appendix for role-level detail)

scenarios.name: Incident Retro — Corrective Actions Summary
scenarios.source_input: RCA report with recommended corrective actions
scenarios.goal: Close the retro by assigning three corrective actions with owners and deadlines
success_notes: Exactly 3 bullets, one per corrective action
Each bullet includes: what, who (owner), and when (deadline)
Headline is action-oriented: 'Three Actions to Prevent Recurrence'
Speaker notes explain the priority order and dependencies between actions
failure_notes: Actions listed without owners or deadlines
More than 3 actions — dilutes focus and accountability
Headline is passive: 'Recommended Actions' instead of directive
Actions are vague ('Improve monitoring') instead of specific ('Add connection pool alert at 80% threshold')

scenarios.name: Product Launch — Title Slide
scenarios.source_input: Product spec (title and date only needed)
scenarios.goal: Open the enablement deck with a clear, branded title slide
success_notes: Headline is the product name: 'Analytics Module Launch' (3-5 words)
Subtitle block includes the context: 'Sales Enablement Kit — April 2026'
Speaker notes welcome the audience and preview the deck's arc (20-30 words)
No bullets, no data, no charts — title slide is clean
failure_notes: Headline is a sentence ('We Are Launching a New Analytics Module')
Subtitle contains bullet points or data
Speaker notes are empty
Uses content.1col instead of title.hero
